item.title: Rise of oxidation and hydration in Neoarchean crust-building magmas caused by subduction (North China Craton)
item.dc:creator: Yuan-Yuan Liu
Chao Wang
Mark B. Allen
Hongyu Zhang
Li Su
Shuguang Song
item.description: The Neoarchean (2.8–2.5 Ga) represents a critical transition towards a habitable Earth. Gradual development of plate subduction during this era facilitated crustal differentiation and maturation, reflected by compositional changes of the Archean continental crust. However, key physical properties of Neoarchean crust-building felsic magmas, such as oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) and water content, remain loosely constrained. Here we show that ca. 2.5 Ga granitoids in the North China Craton are considerably oxidized and hydrous, and comparable to modern arc magmas. Their elevated f O 2 and water content (ΔFMQ −1.0 to 2.8, 5.7–16.6 wt% H 2 O; average at ΔFMQ +0.8 and 9.6 wt% H 2 O) are attributed to fluids released from the subducting oceanic crust, which metasomatized the mantle wedge. Such slab-derived fluids facilitated the generation of Neoarchean oxidized and hydrous felsic magmas through water-fluxed melting of the underplated mafic crust derived from the metasomatized mantle wedge, accompanied by various redox reactions and volatile incorporation. Our work demonstrates that localized synchronous oxidation and extensive fluid circulation across Earth's reservoirs driven by plate subduction likely occurred in some cratons at the end of the Archean. Published: 2026-03-06T23:54:07+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.633 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuan-Yuan Liu; Chao Wang; Mark B. Allen; Hongyu Zhang; Li Su; Shuguang Song

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123359

Relevance: 0.633 (must_read)

The Neoarchean (2.8–2.5 Ga) represents a critical transition towards a habitable Earth. Gradual development of plate subduction during this era facilitated crustal differentiation and maturation, reflected by compositional changes of the Archean continental crust. However, key physical properties of Neoarchean crust-building felsic magmas, such as oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) and water content, remain loosely constrained. Here we show that ca. 2.5 Ga granitoids in the North China Craton are considerably oxidized and hydrous, and comparable to modern arc magmas. Their elevated f O 2 and water content (ΔFMQ −1.0 to 2.8, 5.7–16.6 wt% H 2 O; average at ΔFMQ +0.8 and 9.6 wt% H 2 O) are attributed to fluids released from the subducting oceanic crust, which metasomatized the mantle wedge. Such slab-derived fluids facilitated the generation of Neoarchean oxidized and hydrous felsic magmas through water-fluxed melting of the underplated mafic crust derived from the metasomatized mantle wedge, accompanied by various redox reactions and volatile incorporation. Our work demonstrates that localized synchronous oxidation and extensive fluid circulation across Earth's reservoirs driven by plate subduction likely occurred in some cratons at the end of the Archean.

item.title: The Earliest Cretaceous Intra‐Oceanic Subduction Zone in the Neo‐Tethys: Evidence From an <scp>OIB</scp> ‐Like Intrusion in the Western Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone, Southwestern Tibet
item.dc:creator: Feng Liu
Qiang Liu
Keqing Zong
item.description: ABSTRACT The Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone (ITSZ), a remnant of the Neo‐Tethys in southern Tibet, has been extensively investigated. OIB‐like (Ocean Island Basalt) rocks are widespread in the ITSZ, yet their origin and geodynamic implications remain controversial. This study identified a mafic intrusion in the western ITSZ. Zircon U–Pb dating yielded ages from 142 to 134 Ma, with ε Hf (t) values (−0.7 to +4.5 and −1.9 to +4.3). The alkaline diabases are light rare earth element (LREE)‐enriched, with low ε Nd (t), high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr and radiogenic Pb isotopic ratios, consistent with OIB‐like affinity. These features indicate that such short‐lived magmatism originated from two mantle sources: (1) partial melting of the metasomatized mantle wedge induced by slab‐derived melts, and (2) subsequent asthenosphere upwelling resulting from slab breakoff. Together with the intrusion ages and tectonic setting, these data demonstrate that an intra‐oceanic subduction zone existed within the Neo‐Tethys since at least the earliest Cretaceous. Published: 2026-03-08T09:00:26+00:00 Venue: Terra Nova Score: 0.616 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Feng Liu; Qiang Liu; Keqing Zong

Journal: Terra Nova

DOI: 10.1111/ter.70032

Relevance: 0.616 (must_read)

ABSTRACT The Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone (ITSZ), a remnant of the Neo‐Tethys in southern Tibet, has been extensively investigated. OIB‐like (Ocean Island Basalt) rocks are widespread in the ITSZ, yet their origin and geodynamic implications remain controversial. This study identified a mafic intrusion in the western ITSZ. Zircon U–Pb dating yielded ages from 142 to 134 Ma, with ε Hf (t) values (−0.7 to +4.5 and −1.9 to +4.3). The alkaline diabases are light rare earth element (LREE)‐enriched, with low ε Nd (t), high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr and radiogenic Pb isotopic ratios, consistent with OIB‐like affinity. These features indicate that such short‐lived magmatism originated from two mantle sources: (1) partial melting of the metasomatized mantle wedge induced by slab‐derived melts, and (2) subsequent asthenosphere upwelling resulting from slab breakoff. Together with the intrusion ages and tectonic setting, these data demonstrate that an intra‐oceanic subduction zone existed within the Neo‐Tethys since at least the earliest Cretaceous.

item.title: Mo isotopes in Aleutian sediments: serpentinite-eclogite-sediment mixing and slab depth controls in arc magmas
item.dc:creator: Ekaterina Rojas-Kolomiets
Michael Bizimis
Gene Yogodzinski
Ally Peccia
Terry Plank
Sune Nielsen
item.description: Sediments are considered important sources of Mo for arc magmatism, yet Mo isotope analyses of sediments outboard arc trenches are scarce. We report Mo isotopes ( δ 98/95 Mo) and Mo concentrations for sediments (n = 48) on the Pacific plate, outboard the Aleutian arc from drill cores ODP 886C (n = 19), DSDP 183 (n = 10), IODP 1417 (n = 9) and DSDP 178 (n = 10), and sulfur concentrations in the IODP 1417 samples. With this data, we estimate the mean weighted Mo isotope composition of Aleutian sediments and sediment-derived Mo fluxes along the arc. Additionally, we report Mo isotope systematics on Aleutian lavas from Recheshnoi (n = 3), Makushin (n = 5), Akutan (n = 2), Shishaldin (n = 5) and Frosty Peak (n = 4) to better constrain the Mo systematics in areas east of the Amlia Fracture Zone (AFZ). Aleutian sediments show a wide range of δ 98/95 Mo (–2.0‰ to + 1.2‰) and Mo concentrations (0.2–1121 µg/g) but predominantly low, MORB-like mean weighted δ 98/95 Mo: –0.32‰ (ODP 886C), –0.31‰ (DSDP 183), –0.21‰ (DSDP 178), and + 0.10‰ (IODP 1417). Pelagic sediments with Fe-Mn oxyhydroxides have extremely high Mo concentrations (up to 1121 µg/g), and light δ 98/95 Mo values (–0.46 to –0.19‰). Turbidites with high sulfur concentrations (5000 – 11214 µg/g) have lower Mo concentrations (1.1 – 4.2 µg/g) and heavier Mo isotopes ( δ 98/95 Mo = 0.0 to + 0.32‰), reflecting pyrite precipitation under localized reducing conditions. Aleutian sediments contribute high Mo fluxes (∼200–500 g/(m*yr)) with low δ 98/95 Mo (–0.29‰ to –0.26‰) in the western/central arc and lower fluxes (∼65 g/(m*yr)) with higher δ 98/95 Mo (–0.02‰ to –0.01‰) in the eastern arc. Eastern Aleutian lavas have low δ 98/95 Mo (–0.54‰ to 0.00‰) that further show that high δ 98/95 Mo values (>0‰) are restricted to lavas near the AFZ. Importantly, no bulk sediment can account for these high δ 98/95 Mo values. Instead, from Mo and radiogenic isotopes and trace elements, as well as slab depth observations, the Mo isotope systematics of Aleutian lavas are best explained by a mixture of serpentinite, eclogite and sediments (likely in a mélange style). At the AFZ, the high δ 98/95 Mo can be explained with a higher proportion of the serpentinite component derived from the subduction of the AFZ and shallower slab depths, while in eastern/western lavas with low δ 98/95 Mo partial melting of the subducting plate in eclogitic facies and sediment sources (as well ambient depleted mantle) play a bigger role. Our study shows that Mo isotopes provide a powerful tool to identify source components and the mechanisms of their incorporation into arc magmas. Published: 2026-03-10T16:04:21+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.605 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ekaterina Rojas-Kolomiets; Michael Bizimis; Gene Yogodzinski; Ally Peccia; Terry Plank; Sune Nielsen

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.010

Relevance: 0.605 (must_read)

Sediments are considered important sources of Mo for arc magmatism, yet Mo isotope analyses of sediments outboard arc trenches are scarce. We report Mo isotopes ( δ 98/95 Mo) and Mo concentrations for sediments (n = 48) on the Pacific plate, outboard the Aleutian arc from drill cores ODP 886C (n = 19), DSDP 183 (n = 10), IODP 1417 (n = 9) and DSDP 178 (n = 10), and sulfur concentrations in the IODP 1417 samples. With this data, we estimate the mean weighted Mo isotope composition of Aleutian sediments and sediment-derived Mo fluxes along the arc. Additionally, we report Mo isotope systematics on Aleutian lavas from Recheshnoi (n = 3), Makushin (n = 5), Akutan (n = 2), Shishaldin (n = 5) and Frosty Peak (n = 4) to better constrain the Mo systematics in areas east of the Amlia Fracture Zone (AFZ). Aleutian sediments show a wide range of δ 98/95 Mo (–2.0‰ to + 1.2‰) and Mo concentrations (0.2–1121 µg/g) but predominantly low, MORB-like mean weighted δ 98/95 Mo: –0.32‰ (ODP 886C), –0.31‰ (DSDP 183), –0.21‰ (DSDP 178), and + 0.10‰ (IODP 1417). Pelagic sediments with Fe-Mn oxyhydroxides have extremely high Mo concentrations (up to 1121 µg/g), and light δ 98/95 Mo values (–0.46 to –0.19‰). Turbidites with high sulfur concentrations (5000 – 11214 µg/g) have lower Mo concentrations (1.1 – 4.2 µg/g) and heavier Mo isotopes ( δ 98/95 Mo = 0.0 to + 0.32‰), reflecting pyrite precipitation under localized reducing conditions. Aleutian sediments contribute high Mo fluxes (∼200–500 g/(m*yr)) with low δ 98/95 Mo (–0.29‰ to –0.26‰) in the western/central arc and lower fluxes (∼65 g/(m*yr)) with higher δ 98/95 Mo (–0.02‰ to –0.01‰) in the eastern arc. Eastern Aleutian lavas have low δ 98/95 Mo (–0.54‰ to 0.00‰) that further show that high δ 98/95 Mo values (>0‰) are restricted to lavas near the AFZ. Importantly, no bulk sediment can account for these high δ 98/95 Mo values. Instead, from Mo and radiogenic isotopes and trace elements, as well as slab depth observations, the Mo isotope systematics of Aleutian lavas are best explained by a mixture of serpentinite, eclogite and sediments (likely in a mélange style). At the AFZ, the high δ 98/95 Mo can be explained with a higher proportion of the serpentinite component derived from the subduction of the AFZ and shallower slab depths, while in eastern/western lavas with low δ 98/95 Mo partial melting of the subducting plate in eclogitic facies and sediment sources (as well ambient depleted mantle) play a bigger role. Our study shows that Mo isotopes provide a powerful tool to identify source components and the mechanisms of their incorporation into arc magmas.

item.title: A common mantle source for strongly silica-undersaturated magmas: Insights from Zn-Fe isotopes in kimberlites
item.dc:creator: Ronghua Cai
Andrea Giuliani
Paolo A. Sossi
Tian-Hao Wu
Yongsheng He
D. Graham Pearson
Stephen F. Foley
Jingao Liu
item.description: Kimberlites contain distinct asthenospheric and lithospheric mantle components. Disentangling their relative contributions is a challenging yet important goal to constrain the kimberlite sources. We analyzed the Zn-Fe isotope compositions of 55 globally distributed kimberlites. The Zn-Fe isotopes of kimberlites define a strong positive correlation from isotopically light compositions akin to cratonic peridotites to heavy compositions that are similar to those of melilitites and nephelinites globally. This covariation can be explained by mixing between cratonic mantle peridotites and isotopically similar uncontaminated kimberlite melts, with the observed heavy Zn-Fe isotopic signatures potentially originating from low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric sources. The similarities of trace elements and isotopes between uncontaminated kimberlite melts, nephelinites and melilitites reveal that these strongly silica-undersaturated magmas tap similar mantle sources in the convective mantle. Partial melting of this common source under different melting conditions can produce the spectrum of mantle melts from kimberlitic to silica-undersaturated basaltic melts without necessarily invoking compositional variations in the mantle source. Published: 2026-03-06T14:49:23+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.597 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ronghua Cai; Andrea Giuliani; Paolo A. Sossi; Tian-Hao Wu; Yongsheng He; D. Graham Pearson; Stephen F. Foley; Jingao Liu

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119949

Relevance: 0.597 (must_read)

Kimberlites contain distinct asthenospheric and lithospheric mantle components. Disentangling their relative contributions is a challenging yet important goal to constrain the kimberlite sources. We analyzed the Zn-Fe isotope compositions of 55 globally distributed kimberlites. The Zn-Fe isotopes of kimberlites define a strong positive correlation from isotopically light compositions akin to cratonic peridotites to heavy compositions that are similar to those of melilitites and nephelinites globally. This covariation can be explained by mixing between cratonic mantle peridotites and isotopically similar uncontaminated kimberlite melts, with the observed heavy Zn-Fe isotopic signatures potentially originating from low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric sources. The similarities of trace elements and isotopes between uncontaminated kimberlite melts, nephelinites and melilitites reveal that these strongly silica-undersaturated magmas tap similar mantle sources in the convective mantle. Partial melting of this common source under different melting conditions can produce the spectrum of mantle melts from kimberlitic to silica-undersaturated basaltic melts without necessarily invoking compositional variations in the mantle source.

item.title: The influence of carbonated components on the petrogenesis of the Abaga basalts, Xilin Gol League (Inner Mongolia, China)
item.dc:creator: Jinheng Sun
Ni Li
Yongwei Zhao
Wanfeng Zhang
Jun Hu
Fang Huang
item.description: The Abaga volcanic field of Inner Mongolia, northern China, is situated west of the Daxinganling-Taihangshan gravity lineament and within the eastern part of the Central Asian orogenic belt (the Xing-Meng orogenic belt). This volcanic field consists of alkali basalts and tholeiites. Previous studies reported eruption ages (14.57−2.55 Ma; Luo and Chen, 1990; Ho et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2023) based solely on exposed samples. The range of eruption ages and the chronostratigraphic stages of the study area, based on drill core and outcrop samples, have not been deciphered until now. In addition, the petrogenesis of the tholeiites remains poorly understood. In this study, we present Ar-Ar ages, major and trace element data, and Sr-Nd-Pb-Hf-Mg isotopic values for drill core and outcrop samples from the Abaga basalts. These data are used to identify the eruption stages of this volcanic field, determine whether sedimentary carbonates were present in the magma source, and discuss the genesis of both the alkali basalts and the tholeiites. Based on Ar-Ar dating results and the geological features of the basalts, four eruption stages have been identified. The earliest effusive eruption dates back to 21.38 Ma, which is ∼6.81 m.y. older than previously reported ages. In addition, the Abaga alkali basalts display concentrations of TiO2 (2.57−3.58 wt%) and Na2O + K2O (4.05%−6.36%), along with CaO/Al2O3 (0.58−0.95), Hf/Hf* (0.46−0.83), and Zr/Hf (41.24−47.23) ratios. Their δ26Mg (−0.43‰ to −0.31‰) and δ66Zn (0.47‰−0.50‰; Liu et al., 2022) values are similar to those of carbonated basalts, demonstrating that their magma source was metasomatized by recycled carbonates. The modeling results for δ26Mg and (Zn/Fe) × 104 values illustrate that <10% carbonate was added to the mantle source. Compared to the alkali basalts, the tholeiites display higher Hf/Hf* values (0.78−0.89) and δ26Mg ratios (−0.35‰ to −0.24‰) and lower TiO2 abundances (2.50%−2.91%), CaO/Al2O3 values (0.68−0.85), and Zr/Hf ratios (38.57−42.08). These characteristics suggest that the tholeiites may have been generated by interactions between alkali basalts and the lithosphere. The two-end-member mixing calculation using 143Nd/144Nd and δ26Mg values demonstrates that the addition of 20%−60% lithospheric melt to the alkali basalt can account for the petrogenesis of the tholeiites. Subduction of the Pacific plate likely contributed to the addition of carbonate to the mantle source. In conclusion, these newly reported data provide new insights into the petrogenesis of the basalts. Published: 2026-03-12T18:59:10+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.592 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinheng Sun; Ni Li; Yongwei Zhao; Wanfeng Zhang; Jun Hu; Fang Huang

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38172.1

Relevance: 0.592 (must_read)

The Abaga volcanic field of Inner Mongolia, northern China, is situated west of the Daxinganling-Taihangshan gravity lineament and within the eastern part of the Central Asian orogenic belt (the Xing-Meng orogenic belt). This volcanic field consists of alkali basalts and tholeiites. item.title: Magmatic water variation in the Neo-Tethys: Controlled by convergence rate and slab geometry
item.dc:creator: Shaochen Wu
Rui Wang
Xinyi Zhong
item.description: Water content in magmatic systems exerts a pivotal control on processes from melt generation to eruption, yet the magmatic water variation from subduction to collision and its drivers remain enigmatic. The Gangdese belt in southern Tibet, which archives magmatism from Neo-Tethyan oceanic lithosphere subduction to collision, provides a unique temporal record to address this gap. We apply a fractional crystallization model to both major and trace elements of whole-rock geochemistry data in the Gangdese belt, and we use the difference between amphibole and plagioclase proportions to indicate the variation in magmatic water. Combined with numerical simulations, we link the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys to the change of convergence rate and slab geometry, including normal subduction, slab rollback, and flat-slab subduction. Low convergence rates and slab rollback elevate temperatures, enhancing water release and magmatic hydration, while high convergence rates and flat-slab subduction cool the system, limiting slab dehydration. From 215 Ma to 160 Ma, shifting slab geometry from normal to rollback to flat-slab matched with magmatic water variability, whereas 160−15 Ma variations correlated with convergence rate changes. Using the fractional crystallization model, we obtain the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys subduction zone. We further explore its connection to the change of subduction parameters, advancing understanding of subduction zone water cycles and their broader implications for arc magmatism. Published: 2026-03-09T18:23:45+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.592 (must_read)

item.title: High-titanium mare basalts: From petrogenesis to lunar evolution
item.dc:creator: Haojie Chen
Bin Su
Yi Chen
Sheng Gou
Hengci Tian
Fu-Yuan Wu
item.description: High-Ti mare basalts, characterized by their unique titanium-rich compositions (TiO2 contents up to 16 wt.%), provide critical insights into lunar magma ocean (LMO) differentiation, the structure and composition of the lunar mantle, and lunar internal dynamics. Here we systematically synthesize the spatiotemporal distribution, petrology, mineralogy, and geochemistry of high-Ti mare basalts. By integrating these compiled data with thermodynamic modeling, we present a comprehensive evaluation of the four prevailing petrogenetic models: (1) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) remelting, (2) IBC assimilation, (3) reaction of IBC-derived melts with early LMO cumulates, and (4) mantle source hybridization. Our analyses confirm that the distinct compositional characteristics of high-Ti mare basalts were inherited from late-stage, ilmenite-bearing LMO cumulates. However, we find that the IBC direct remelting, assimilation, and melt-rock reaction models (Models 1–3) each face significant limitations, failing to reconcile observed cumulate components, reproduce the full spectrum of melt compositions, or satisfy thermodynamic viability. In contrast, the mantle hybridization model (Model 4) emerges as the most promising mechanism, successfully satisfying multiple experimental and observational constraints for lunar high-Ti magmatism. We suggest that high-Ti mare basalts are not simple melting products of a single cumulate source. Instead, they represent the combined products of lunar mantle overturn, cumulate hybridization, and partial melting during the Moon’s long-term evolution. This scenario underscores a lunar interior with a far more complex physicochemical state than traditionally envisioned. Future research should prioritize constraining high-Ti mare basalts’ specific source composition and depth of origin, the mechanisms driving their distinct two-stage eruptive history (3.5–3.9 Ga and <2.0 Ga), and their spatial distribution patterns. Resolving these questions is paramount for understanding LMO differentiation, mantle overturn, the Moon’s thermal evolution, and the origin of the lunar hemispheric dichotomy. Published: 2026-03-09T07:53:29+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.590 (must_read)

Instead, they represent the combined products of lunar mantle overturn, cumulate hybridization, and partial melting during the Moon’s long-term evolution. This scenario underscores a lunar interior with a far more complex physicochemical state than traditionally envisioned. Resolving these questions is paramount for understanding LMO differentiation, mantle overturn, the Moon’s thermal evolution, and the origin of the lunar hemispheric dichotomy.

item.title: Subduction Zone Magnetism: The Influence of Metamorphism and Serpentinization in the Mantle Wedge
item.dc:creator: Y. Li
P. C. Lindquist
D. Han
B. Fish
R. J. Harrison
W. McCarthy
R. M. Palin
C. B. Condit
C. I. O. Nichols
item.description: Abstract Subduction zone magnetic anomalies have previously been used to infer their thermal structure assuming a uniformly serpentinized mantle carries a homogeneous, isotropic magnetization. However, seismic tomography, geological observations and numerical modeling provide increasing evidence for a non‐uniformly serpentinized mantle wedge that may carry heterogeneous magnetization. In this study, we characterize the rock magnetic properties in variably serpentinized samples from Santa Catalina Island/Pimu'nga in California, USA, and uncover at least two populations of magnetite formed during metamorphism and serpentinization of the mantle wedge. All samples contain Cr‐magnetite, which is believed to form through the amphibolite facies metamorphism of Cr‐spinel under high fluid:rock ratios, as opposed to serpentinization reactions, which have been invoked for mantle wedge magnetism in the past. Heavily serpentinized samples contain stoichiometric magnetite associated with serpentinization reactions under wedge conditions. We propose that the observed magnetic anomalies above subduction zones are primarily controlled by two factors: (a) the extent of fluids available for metamorphism of the mantle wedge and the transformation of Cr‐spinel to Cr‐magnetite and (b) the extent of subsequent serpentinization which may generate a layer of heavily serpentinized rock containing a mixture of Cr‐ and stoichiometric magnetite at the base of the mantle wedge. This proposal precludes the direct connection between subduction zone magnetic anomalies and thermal structure without prior characterization of fluid availability for both metamorphism and serpentinization, and its implications on wedge magnetic mineralogy. Published: 2026-03-09T03:43:49+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.589 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Y. Li; P. C. Lindquist; D. Han; B. Fish; R. J. Harrison; W. McCarthy; R. M. Palin; C. B. Condit; C. I. O. Nichols

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012354

Relevance: 0.589 (must_read)

Abstract Subduction zone magnetic anomalies have previously been used to infer their thermal structure assuming a uniformly serpentinized mantle carries a homogeneous, isotropic magnetization. However, seismic tomography, geological observations and numerical modeling provide increasing evidence for a non‐uniformly serpentinized mantle wedge that may carry heterogeneous magnetization. In this study, we characterize the rock magnetic properties in variably serpentinized samples from Santa Catalina Island/Pimu'nga in California, USA, and uncover at least two populations of magnetite formed during metamorphism and serpentinization of the mantle wedge. All samples contain Cr‐magnetite, which is believed to form through the amphibolite facies metamorphism of Cr‐spinel under high fluid:rock ratios, as opposed to serpentinization reactions, which have been invoked for mantle wedge magnetism in the past. Heavily serpentinized samples contain stoichiometric magnetite associated with serpentinization reactions under wedge conditions. We propose that the observed magnetic anomalies above subduction zones are primarily controlled by two factors: (a) the extent of fluids available for metamorphism of the mantle wedge and the transformation of Cr‐spinel to Cr‐magnetite and (b) the extent of subsequent serpentinization which may generate a layer of heavily serpentinized rock containing a mixture of Cr‐ and stoichiometric magnetite at the base of the mantle wedge. This proposal precludes the direct connection between subduction zone magnetic anomalies and thermal structure without prior characterization of fluid availability for both metamorphism and serpentinization, and its implications on wedge magnetic mineralogy.

item.title: Melt-depleted plume mantle sourced the oldest Atlantic Ocean crust
item.dc:creator: Yuan Zhong
Sanzhong Li
Lihang Peng
Lijun Liu
Li-Hui Chen
Xiao-Hui Li
Jun Hu
item.description: Mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORBs) are known to sample the heterogeneous uppermost mantle, providing insights into the mantle’s evolution. Early oceanic crust may have documented information about the nature of convecting mantle during the breakup of continent and the opening of ocean. This study examined MORBs from various Deep Sea Drilling Project sites at the Central Atlantic, spanning ages from 160 to 80 Ma. Systematic differences in chemical compositions emerge between the oldest (>140 Ma) and younger (<140 Ma) basalts. The oldest Atlantic crust demonstrate pronounced reduction in rare earth element abundances, yet are anomalously enriched in Sr, as well as more enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotope compositions relative to the younger, more normal-MORB-like crust. Geochemical and isotopic data from the oldest Atlantic crust indicate the presence of plume materials emplaced beneath the young Atlantic Ridge. These plume materials have undergone a complex evolution, involving ancient melt extraction likely related to the magmatism of Central Atlantic Magmatic Province, subsequent re-enrichment by percolating melts, and then remelting at Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The results demonstrate that the compositions of the oldest Atlantic MORBs reflect dynamic mantle processes rather than simple binary mixing between homogeneous depleted and enriched end-members. Our findings highlight the role of melt-depleted lithology played in mid-ocean ridge magmatism and shaping the compositional heterogeneity in the convecting mantle throughout geological time. Published: 2026-03-12T10:46:11+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.589 (consider)

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119962

Relevance: 0.589 (consider)

Mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORBs) are known to sample the heterogeneous uppermost mantle, providing insights into the mantle’s evolution. Early oceanic crust may have documented information about the nature of convecting mantle during the breakup of continent and the opening of ocean. This study examined MORBs from various Deep Sea Drilling Project sites at the Central Atlantic, spanning ages from 160 to 80 Ma. Systematic differences in chemical compositions emerge between the oldest (>140 Ma) and younger (<140 Ma) basalts. The oldest Atlantic crust demonstrate pronounced reduction in rare earth element abundances, yet are anomalously enriched in Sr, as well as more enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotope compositions relative to the younger, more normal-MORB-like crust. Geochemical and isotopic data from the oldest Atlantic crust indicate the presence of plume materials emplaced beneath the young Atlantic Ridge. These plume materials have undergone a complex evolution, involving ancient melt extraction likely related to the magmatism of Central Atlantic Magmatic Province, subsequent re-enrichment by percolating melts, and then remelting at Mid-Atlantic Ridge. The results demonstrate that the compositions of the oldest Atlantic MORBs reflect dynamic mantle processes rather than simple binary mixing between homogeneous depleted and enriched end-members. Our findings highlight the role of melt-depleted lithology played in mid-ocean ridge magmatism and shaping the compositional heterogeneity in the convecting mantle throughout geological time.

item.title: Evidence for organic and igneous carbon sources in the Mesoproterozoic Adirondack Highlands graphite district, New York
item.dc:creator: William H. Peck
Jade Star Lackey
Gregory J. Walsh
Katia A. Childs
Matthew C. King
Fredrick S. LeClair
Jacob L. Steinberg
item.description: This study examined the origin of graphite mineralization in the Mesoproterozoic Adirondack Highlands. Flake graphite was mined from the historic mining district around Ticonderoga, New York, USA, from the 1850s to the 1920s. Most of the production and reserves from the district are disseminated graphite from feldspathic quartz schists (e.g., Dixon-American Graphite Co. mine, Faxon property, Flake Graphite Company). Protolith sediments of the schists were deposited in the Trans-Adirondack backarc basin at ca. 1.25 Ga and were metamorphosed to high grade at 1.18−1.15 Ga during the accretionary Shawinigan orogeny and at 1.09−1.02 Ga during the Ottawan phase of the Grenvillian orogeny. Major elements and carbon isotopes of graphite from schists (δ13C = −28.1‰ to −5.4‰ relative to Vienna Peedee belemnite) are consistent with derivation from organic carbon in protolith sandstones that devolatilized during metamorphism. Some small mines and prospects in the district mined graphitic calcite marbles and clinopyroxene ± scapolite skarns associated with a pegmatite suite dated by U-Pb in zircon to 1.04−1.01 Ga (e.g., Lead Hill and Crown Point deposits), and rare graphite veins are observed at several deposits. Hydrothermal flake graphite from pegmatites, skarns, and veins are distinct from graphite in schist-hosted deposits and have δ13C values that cluster around −7‰ to −5‰, consistent with deposition from igneous CO2. Covariation of carbon and oxygen stable isotopes and U-Pb zircon geochronology are not compatible with precipitation from fluids derived from country rock metapelites or marbles. Some graphite districts elsewhere show compelling evidence for remobilization and redeposition of sedimentary-derived carbon as hydrothermal graphite, sometimes as thick veins producing world-class, high-grade deposits. In the Adirondacks, there is evidence for two generations of graphite mineralization with distinct carbon sources: syngenetic graphite with an organic origin in Shawinigan quartz schists and epigenetic graphite with an igneous origin in Ottawan skarns and pegmatites. Published: 2026-03-12T18:59:10+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.588 (consider)

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

This study examined the origin of graphite mineralization in the Mesoproterozoic Adirondack Highlands. Flake graphite was mined from the historic mining district around Ticonderoga, New York, USA, from the 1850s to the 1920s. Most of the production and reserves from the district are disseminated graphite from feldspathic quartz schists (e.g., Dixon-American Graphite Co. mine, Faxon property, Flake Graphite Company). Protolith sediments of the schists were deposited in the Trans-Adirondack backarc basin at ca. 1.25 Ga and were metamorphosed to high grade at 1.18−1.15 Ga during the accretionary Shawinigan orogeny and at 1.09−1.02 Ga during the Ottawan phase of the Grenvillian orogeny. Major elements and carbon isotopes of graphite from schists (δ13C = −28.1‰ to −5.4‰ relative to Vienna Peedee belemnite) are consistent with derivation from organic carbon in protolith sandstones that devolatilized during metamorphism. Some small mines and prospects in the district mined graphitic calcite marbles and clinopyroxene ± scapolite skarns associated with a pegmatite suite dated by U-Pb in zircon to 1.04−1.01 Ga (e.g., Lead Hill and Crown Point deposits), and rare graphite veins are observed at several deposits. Hydrothermal flake graphite from pegmatites, skarns, and veins are distinct from graphite in schist-hosted deposits and have δ13C values that cluster around −7‰ to −5‰, consistent with deposition from igneous CO2. Covariation of carbon and oxygen stable isotopes and U-Pb zircon geochronology are not compatible with precipitation from fluids derived from country rock metapelites or marbles. Some graphite districts elsewhere show compelling evidence for remobilization and redeposition of sedimentary-derived carbon as hydrothermal graphite, sometimes as thick veins producing world-class, high-grade deposits. In the Adirondacks, there is evidence for two generations of graphite mineralization with distinct carbon sources: syngenetic graphite with an organic origin in Shawinigan quartz schists and epigenetic graphite with an igneous origin in Ottawan skarns and pegmatites.

item.title: Blueschist Dislocation Creep and Glide in Subduction Systems: Constraints From Glaucophane Experiments
item.dc:creator: Lonnie J. Hufford
Leif Tokle
Whitney M. Behr
Luiz F. G. Morales
Claudio Madonna
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Blueschists are a defining component of subducting oceanic crust and influence slab and interface rheology across thermal regimes. Glaucophane, the dominant sodic amphibole in blueschist, is a key rheology‐controlling phase, yet its dislocation‐based strength is poorly constrained. We conducted load‐stepping experiments in a Griggs apparatus at 600–700C and 1.0 GPa over shear strain rates from ∼ to 1.2, using a starting grain size of n = 3, Q = 450 15 kJ/mol, A = 2.32 × and dislocation glide A = , = 22 GPa, H = 800 kJ/mol, p = 1, and q = 2. Extrapolation to geologic conditions suggests that dislocation glide will not operate under steady‐state subduction‐interface conditions, and is likely only relevant during transient high stress perturbations. Dislocation creep dominates at temperatures above 450°C at grain sizes of ∼1 mm or larger. Comparison with published quartz/metasediment and eclogite flow laws indicates that blueschist strengths are intermediate between these endmembers, implying a meaningful contribution to long‐term interface strength in mafic‐dominated (sediment‐poor) margins. Together, these results refine glaucophane‐controlled blueschist rheology and its implications for subduction zone mechanics. Published: 2026-03-09T11:24:21+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lonnie J. Hufford; Leif Tokle; Whitney M. Behr; Luiz F. G. Morales; Claudio Madonna

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb033622

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

Abstract Blueschists are a defining component of subducting oceanic crust and influence slab and interface rheology across thermal regimes. Glaucophane, the dominant sodic amphibole in blueschist, is a key rheology‐controlling phase, yet its dislocation‐based strength is poorly constrained. We conducted load‐stepping experiments in a Griggs apparatus at 600–700C and 1.0 GPa over shear strain rates from ∼ to 1.2, using a starting grain size of n = 3, Q = 450 15 kJ/mol, A = 2.32 × and dislocation glide A = , = 22 GPa, H = 800 kJ/mol, p = 1, and q = 2. Extrapolation to geologic conditions suggests that dislocation glide will not operate under steady‐state subduction‐interface conditions, and is likely only relevant during transient high stress perturbations. Dislocation creep dominates at temperatures above 450°C at grain sizes of ∼1 mm or larger. Comparison with published quartz/metasediment and eclogite flow laws indicates that blueschist strengths are intermediate between these endmembers, implying a meaningful contribution to long‐term interface strength in mafic‐dominated (sediment‐poor) margins. Together, these results refine glaucophane‐controlled blueschist rheology and its implications for subduction zone mechanics.

item.title: Electrical Conductivity of Amorphous and Molten CaCO <sub>3</sub> at High Pressures and Its Implications for Mantle Conductivity Anomalies
item.dc:creator: Bin Zhao
Takashi Yoshino
Qi Chen
Tony Yu
Dongzhou Zhang
Bin Chen
Yanbin Wang
item.description: Abstract Impedance spectrometry experiments have been conducted on CaCO 3 up to 15 GPa and 2,100 K to identify its state under high pressure. The melting temperature of CaCO 3 was also determined by the falling of a Re sphere observed via X‐ray radiography. The phase transition from aragonite to the amorphous phase does not cause a leap in the Electrical conductivity (EC), while a drastic increase in the EC, by 1.5–2.0 log units, only occurs with the onset of melting. The EC of amorphous CaCO 3 is comparable to other hydrous mantle minerals at similar pressure and temperature conditions. The required fraction of amorphous CaCO 3 implies that it can be excluded from the potential origins responsible for the observed high EC anomalies in the upper mantle. If the conductivity anomalies are induced by the presence of carbonate, a low‐degree melting of carbonate‐bearing peridotite is anticipated. Published: 2026-03-08T13:44:01+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.581 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bin Zhao; Takashi Yoshino; Qi Chen; Tony Yu; Dongzhou Zhang; Bin Chen; Yanbin Wang

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl119568

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

Abstract Impedance spectrometry experiments have been conducted on CaCO 3 up to 15 GPa and 2,100 K to identify its state under high pressure. The melting temperature of CaCO 3 was also determined by the falling of a Re sphere observed via X‐ray radiography. The phase transition from aragonite to the amorphous phase does not cause a leap in the Electrical conductivity (EC), while a drastic increase in the EC, by 1.5–2.0 log units, only occurs with the onset of melting. The EC of amorphous CaCO 3 is comparable to other hydrous mantle minerals at similar pressure and temperature conditions. The required fraction of amorphous CaCO 3 implies that it can be excluded from the potential origins responsible for the observed high EC anomalies in the upper mantle. If the conductivity anomalies are induced by the presence of carbonate, a low‐degree melting of carbonate‐bearing peridotite is anticipated.

item.title: Melting of fluorine-rich biotite as a mechanism for generating lithium-rich granites
item.dc:creator: Matthew C. Morris
Owen M. Weller
Caroline R. Soderman
Marie Edmonds
Charles D. Beard
Christopher M. Yeomans
item.description: Abstract Granitic melts derived from anatexis of metasedimentary rocks, particularly biotite-dehydration reactions, are important lithium (Li) sources. Petrogenetic models depend on mineral-melt lithium partitioning, yet published partition coefficients ( $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt ) vary by over an order of magnitude, and are commonly used as static values. Here we use thermodynamic modelling coupled with relevant published $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt ranges, including a dynamic composition- and temperature-dependent $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{biotite/melt}}}}}$$ D Li biotite/melt , to quantify viable enrichment during partial melting and fractional crystallisation. Using the lithium-rich Cornubian granite batholith, we demonstrate the sensitivity of results to $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt choices for modally-dominant lithium-poor phases (e.g. quartz), as well as phases traditionally thought to dominate lithium budgets (e.g. biotite). While economic lithium enrichment can result from extreme fractionation with selective $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt , we suggest that dehydration melting of fluorinated biotite is the most viable petrogenetic model. The latter reconciles the common observations of fluorite in lithium-granites and their late-orogenic occurrence, and provides a mechanism for extensive fractionation. Published: 2026-03-09T13:56:44+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.579 (consider)

Authors: Matthew C. Morris; Owen M. Weller; Caroline R. Soderman; Marie Edmonds; Charles D. Beard; Christopher M. Yeomans

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03361-x

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Abstract Granitic melts derived from anatexis of metasedimentary rocks, particularly biotite-dehydration reactions, are important lithium (Li) sources. Petrogenetic models depend on mineral-melt lithium partitioning, yet published partition coefficients ( $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt ) vary by over an order of magnitude, and are commonly used as static values. Here we use thermodynamic modelling coupled with relevant published $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt ranges, including a dynamic composition- and temperature-dependent $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{biotite/melt}}}}}$$ D Li biotite/melt , to quantify viable enrichment during partial melting and fractional crystallisation. Using the lithium-rich Cornubian granite batholith, we demonstrate the sensitivity of results to $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt choices for modally-dominant lithium-poor phases (e.g. quartz), as well as phases traditionally thought to dominate lithium budgets (e.g. biotite). While economic lithium enrichment can result from extreme fractionation with selective $${D}_{{{{\rm{Li}}}}}^{{{{\rm{mineral/melt}}}}}$$ D Li mineral/melt , we suggest that dehydration melting of fluorinated biotite is the most viable petrogenetic model. The latter reconciles the common observations of fluorite in lithium-granites and their late-orogenic occurrence, and provides a mechanism for extensive fractionation.

item.title: Critical metal enrichment in volcanic-influenced Permian basins: New insights from the Emeishan Large Igneous Province, southwest China
item.dc:creator: Zhuangzhuang Bao
Shifeng Dai
Victor P. Nechaev
Xiaofang He
David French
Ian Graham
Sergey Y. Budnitskiy
Zhengfu Zhao
item.description: The enrichment of Nb, Zr, Ga, and rare earth elements and yttrium (REY) in sedimentary basins represents a critical, yet poorly constrained component of the global metal cycle. In southwest China, Late Permian coal-bearing strata host Nb-Zr-REY-Ga-rich mudstones, but their metal sources remain debated. Previous studies have primarily attributed this enrichment to either weathered residues of Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP) basalts or alkaline volcaniclastics. Mineralogical and geochemical data from two drill cores of the Longtan Formation in Zhenxiong, eastern Yunnan, China, combined with zircon U-Pb-Hf and whole-rock Sr-Nd isotopic analyses of the mineralized horizons, were used to develop a source-to-sink model for their temporal and spatial evolution. The major- and trace-element characteristics of the mineralized beds (e.g., Al2O3/TiO2, Eu/Eu*, Nb/Yb, and REY distribution patterns) are comparable to ELIP alkaline rocks, whereas non-mineralized beds are closer to ELIP basalts. Zircon U-Pb ages from the mineralized beds cluster at 260.9−257.4 Ma, consistent with late-stage ELIP magmatism, while a small number of older detrital zircons are mainly Neoproterozoic in age. The former records εHf(t) values ranging from −15.71 to +11.37 and TDM1 model ages of 1498−438 Ma, overall showing within-plate affinities, with only a minor arc-related zircon population that may reflect hydrothermal alteration. Whole-rock 87Sr/86Sr(t) values of 0.70259−0.70606 and εNd(t) values of −3.58 to +1.75 show ranges comparable to those of Nb-Ta-mineralized alkaline rocks in the ELIP interior [87Sr/86Sr(t) = 0.70004−0.70424; εNd(t) = 0.4−2.7]. The mineralized mudstones reflect a mixed provenance, dominated by alkaline volcaniclastic input during late-stage ELIP activity, with additional contributions from weathered Precambrian basement rocks beneath ELIP volcanic edifices and, to a lesser extent, Neoproterozoic uplands along the Yangtze Block margins. A minor input also came from contemporaneous arc-related volcanism linked to Paleo-Tethyan subduction, which may have increased upward through the Permian sequence. These results highlight that the enrichment of critical metals in volcanic-influenced sedimentary basins was primarily driven by plume-related alkaline volcaniclastics, with additional contributions from crustal and arc sources. Published: 2026-03-12T18:59:16+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.578 (consider)

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38528.1

Relevance: 0.578 (consider)

The enrichment of Nb, Zr, Ga, and rare earth elements and yttrium (REY) in sedimentary basins represents a critical, yet poorly constrained component of the global metal cycle. In southwest China, Late Permian coal-bearing strata host Nb-Zr-REY-Ga-rich mudstones, but their metal sources remain debated. The major- and trace-element characteristics of the mineralized beds (e.g., Al2O3/TiO2, Eu/Eu*, Nb/Yb, and REY distribution patterns) are comparable to ELIP alkaline rocks, whereas non-mineralized beds are closer to ELIP basalts. Zircon U-Pb ages from the mineralized beds cluster at 260.9−257.4 Ma, consistent with late-stage ELIP magmatism, while a small number of older detrital zircons are mainly Neoproterozoic in age. The former records εHf(t) values ranging from −15.71 to +11.37 and TDM1 model ages of 1498−438 Ma, overall showing within-plate affinities, with only a minor arc-related zircon population that may reflect hydrothermal alteration. Whole-rock 87Sr/86Sr(t) values of 0.70259−0.70606 and εNd(t) values of −3.58 to +1.75 show ranges comparable to those of Nb-Ta-mineralized alkaline rocks in the ELIP interior [87Sr/86Sr(t) = 0.70004−0.70424; εNd(t) = 0.4−2.7]. A minor input also came from contemporaneous arc-related volcanism linked to Paleo-Tethyan subduction, which may have increased upward through the Permian sequence. These results highlight that the enrichment of critical metals in volcanic-influenced sedimentary basins was primarily driven by plume-related alkaline volcaniclastics, with additional contributions from crustal and arc sources.

item.title: A central position for the South China Block in Rodinia: New evidence from a non-subduction origin of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode mafic–ultramafic rocks
item.dc:creator: Chutian Shu
Xi Zhu
Zheng-Xiang Li
Jingyu Wang
Zhuoying Zhang
Xiaoping Long
item.description: The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. The magmatism has been interpreted either as subduction-related, placing South China on the margin of Rodinia, or as plume-related, suggesting a central position linking Australia and Laurentia. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Coeval plume-related magmatism is also documented elsewhere within the South China Block beyond the Sibao orogen, e.g., the Bikou flood basalts, further supporting a regional-scale thermal anomaly. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia. Published: 2026-03-09T16:40:39+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.577 (consider)

The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia.

item.title: Fluid transport and reaction during disseminated mineralization in orogenic gold systems
item.dc:creator: Peng-Cong Zhang
Hao-Cheng Yu
Luca Smeraglia
Yong-Bao Gao
Chao Li
Jia-Nan Fu
Zhao-Hua Bai
Rui Wang
Kun-Feng Qiu
item.description: Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems. Published: 2026-03-09T18:23:49+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.576 (consider)

Authors: Peng-Cong Zhang; Hao-Cheng Yu; Luca Smeraglia; Yong-Bao Gao; Chao Li; Jia-Nan Fu; Zhao-Hua Bai; Rui Wang; Kun-Feng Qiu

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38735.1

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems.

item.title: Interaction between ilmenite-bearing cumulates and lunar mantle: implications for the origin of intermediate and high-titanium lunar basalts
item.dc:creator: Himela Moitra
Sujoy Ghosh
Tamalkanti Mukherjee
Saibal Gupta
Kuljeet Kaur Marhas
item.description: High-pressure layered and mixed experiments were performed at pressures of 1–3 GPa and temperatures of 1075–1500 °C in a piston cylinder apparatus to study the interaction between a novel (fayalite-rich) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) composition and ambient lunar mantle (represented by San Carlos olivine with X Mg of 0.91), to investigate the origin of intermediate- and high-Ti mare basalts (6–18 wt% TiO 2 ). The partial melts generated in the layered experiments show TiO 2 contents (9–19 wt%) similar to high-Ti mare basalts, but low MgO ( 2 ) of the IBC layer can directly fractionate to generate intermediate-Ti lunar basalts. Lower-temperature, high-Ti partial melts (11–19 wt% TiO 2 ) may first fractionate to more Ti-rich, Mg-poor compositions, then mix with ascending low-Ti, picritic magmas and undergo subsequent fractionation during ascent to generate high-Ti basalt compositions. Our model successfully reproduces the observed evolution of MgO, TiO 2 , SiO 2 , and FeO in high-Ti basalts, although it underestimates their Al 2 O 3 and CaO contents. Incorporation of pockets of plagioclase-rich melts from the mantle may resolve this discrepancy. High-pressure density calculations suggest that at 1 GPa, high-Ti partial melts can ascend to the lunar surface. At 2–3 GPa, some partial melts may ascend to the surface after fractionation and assimilation, while some may sink through the lunar mantle. These findings provide new insights on the role of lunar mantle overturn, IBC melting and mantle interactions in the formation of lunar high-Ti basalts and offer a viable mechanism for their generation throughout the history of mare volcanism. Published: 2026-03-11T00:21:33+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Himela Moitra; Sujoy Ghosh; Tamalkanti Mukherjee; Saibal Gupta; Kuljeet Kaur Marhas

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.008

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

High-pressure layered and mixed experiments were performed at pressures of 1–3 GPa and temperatures of 1075–1500 °C in a piston cylinder apparatus to study the interaction between a novel (fayalite-rich) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) composition and ambient lunar mantle (represented by San Carlos olivine with X Mg of 0.91), to investigate the origin of intermediate- and high-Ti mare basalts (6–18 wt% TiO 2 ). The partial melts generated in the layered experiments show TiO 2 contents (9–19 wt%) similar to high-Ti mare basalts, but low MgO ( 2 ) of the IBC layer can directly fractionate to generate intermediate-Ti lunar basalts. Lower-temperature, high-Ti partial melts (11–19 wt% TiO 2 ) may first fractionate to more Ti-rich, Mg-poor compositions, then mix with ascending low-Ti, picritic magmas and undergo subsequent fractionation during ascent to generate high-Ti basalt compositions. Our model successfully reproduces the observed evolution of MgO, TiO 2 , SiO 2 , and FeO in high-Ti basalts, although it underestimates their Al 2 O 3 and CaO contents. Incorporation of pockets of plagioclase-rich melts from the mantle may resolve this discrepancy. High-pressure density calculations suggest that at 1 GPa, high-Ti partial melts can ascend to the lunar surface. At 2–3 GPa, some partial melts may ascend to the surface after fractionation and assimilation, while some may sink through the lunar mantle. These findings provide new insights on the role of lunar mantle overturn, IBC melting and mantle interactions in the formation of lunar high-Ti basalts and offer a viable mechanism for their generation throughout the history of mare volcanism.

item.title: Diffusive copper redistribution from shallow degassed magmas to mafic enclaves. Insight from anomalously Cu-enriched enclaves from Bezymianny volcano, Kamchatka
item.dc:creator: Vesta O. Davydova
Vasily D. Shcherbakov
Pavel Yu. Plechov
Vasily O. Yapaskurt
Yuri D. Shcherbakov
Alexander B. Perepelov
Nikolay V. Brianskii
Viktor S. Antipin
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research
item.description: Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks. Published: 2026-03-09T16:47:29+00:00 Venue: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vesta O. Davydova; Vasily D. Shcherbakov; Pavel Yu. Plechov; Vasily O. Yapaskurt; Yuri D. Shcherbakov; Alexander B. Perepelov; Nikolay V. Brianskii; Viktor S. Antipin

Journal: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.jvolgeores.2026.108588

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks.

item.title: Metamorphism and chronology of Neoproterozoic granulite facies metasedimentary rock in northwestern Yangtze Block and its tectonic implications
item.dc:creator: Linfeng Feng
Haozheng Wang
Byung Choon Lee
Jinghao Fu
Yi Zhang
Heng Liu
Meimei Han
Bo Hu
Xiahong Cui
Mingguo Zhai
item.description: The role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia Supercontinent is still debated. The garnet-biotite metatexite in the Micangshan area, northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block, mainly consists of garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, and quartz with minor ilmenite and accessory minerals of zircon and monazite. The prograde-stage is inclusions inside the garnet, including quartz, biotite, plagioclase, and monazite. The peak-stage is characterized by equilibrium garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, quartz, and ilmenite. The retrograde-stage represents the anhedral small flaky biotite and the coexistence of minor anhedral plagioclase and K-feldspar. Combining the P-T estimation from the pseudosection by Perple_X, quartz-in-garnet (QuiG) Raman-barometer, and conventional geothermobarometers, the prograde-stage P-T condition is constrained at 477–555°C, 5.0–6.5 kbar, and the peak and retrograde stages were determined as 800–830°C, 7–8.8 kbar and 603–674°C, 4.5–5.5 kbar, respectively. A clockwise P-T path was constructed. Metamorphic zircons in this rock yield metamorphic ages of 808 ± 5 Ma and 728–746 Ma. Zircons formed at ca. 808 Ma revealed a crystallization temperature of 727 ± 12°C, which is lower than the peak-stage temperature. Thus, zircons were regarded as retrograde products. Inherited zircons in this rock yielded 206 Pb/ 238 U apparent dates of 828–939 Ma. These zircons show geochemical characteristics of orogenic or arc-related origins. The 206 Pb/ 238 U dates of the monazite span in the range of 788–822 Ma, which suggests two centres at 817 Ma and 803 Ma. As monazite hosted in the garnet, it could record the metamorphic history from the prograde. Thus, 817 Ma is believed to be the time of peak-stage metamorphism, and the 808–788 Ma is interpreted as retrograde time. Based on all of the above, the northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block is suggested to have experienced the collision process, resulting in granulite facies metamorphism at ca. 817 Ma. This study provides additional constraints to decode the role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia evolution. Published: 2026-03-06T05:30:04+00:00 Venue: International Geology Review Score: 0.575 (consider)

Authors: Linfeng Feng; Haozheng Wang; Byung Choon Lee; Jinghao Fu; Yi Zhang; Heng Liu; Meimei Han; Bo Hu; Xiahong Cui; Mingguo Zhai

Journal: International Geology Review

DOI: 10.1080/00206814.2026.2635584

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

The role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia Supercontinent is still debated. The garnet-biotite metatexite in the Micangshan area, northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block, mainly consists of garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, and quartz with minor ilmenite and accessory minerals of zircon and monazite. The prograde-stage is inclusions inside the garnet, including quartz, biotite, plagioclase, and monazite. The peak-stage is characterized by equilibrium garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, quartz, and ilmenite. The retrograde-stage represents the anhedral small flaky biotite and the coexistence of minor anhedral plagioclase and K-feldspar. Combining the P-T estimation from the pseudosection by Perple_X, quartz-in-garnet (QuiG) Raman-barometer, and conventional geothermobarometers, the prograde-stage P-T condition is constrained at 477–555°C, 5.0–6.5 kbar, and the peak and retrograde stages were determined as 800–830°C, 7–8.8 kbar and 603–674°C, 4.5–5.5 kbar, respectively. A clockwise P-T path was constructed. Metamorphic zircons in this rock yield metamorphic ages of 808 ± 5 Ma and 728–746 Ma. Zircons formed at ca. 808 Ma revealed a crystallization temperature of 727 ± 12°C, which is lower than the peak-stage temperature. Thus, zircons were regarded as retrograde products. Inherited zircons in this rock yielded 206 Pb/ 238 U apparent dates of 828–939 Ma. These zircons show geochemical characteristics of orogenic or arc-related origins. The 206 Pb/ 238 U dates of the monazite span in the range of 788–822 Ma, which suggests two centres at 817 Ma and 803 Ma. As monazite hosted in the garnet, it could record the metamorphic history from the prograde. Thus, 817 Ma is believed to be the time of peak-stage metamorphism, and the 808–788 Ma is interpreted as retrograde time. Based on all of the above, the northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block is suggested to have experienced the collision process, resulting in granulite facies metamorphism at ca. 817 Ma. This study provides additional constraints to decode the role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia evolution.

item.title: 3‐D Modeling of Differential Exhumation of Ultrahigh‐Pressure Metamorphic Rocks Driven by Increasing Plate Divergence
item.dc:creator: Xinxin Wang
Liang Zhao
Marco G. Malusà
Weiqiang Ji
Jie Yuan
item.description: Abstract Petrological and seismic constraints suggest differential sampling depth for ultrahigh pressure (UHP) metamorphic rocks exposed in different segments of the fossil subduction zone of the Western Alps. However, the mechanisms for the observed differential exhumation remain to be understood. Here, we account for the continental margin subduction within double subduction systems coupled with three‐dimensional (3‐D) geodynamic models to investigate the potential for this phenomenon. We tested two end‐member scenarios of upper‐plate motion, including Adria counterclockwise rotation and divergent motion away from the trench. Results show that both scenarios can produce a unidirectional increase in the metamorphic peak of exhumed UHP rocks along the orogen strike. Only in the simulations where the counterclockwise rotation of the upper plate acts as the primary driver of exhumation did the resulting characteristics of the deep structure fit with those observed in the Western Alps. They include the presence of an exhumed mantle wedge beneath the southern UHP domes and its absence beneath the northern UHP domes. Our results can be exported to other subduction zones where kinematic constraints suggest a component of upper‐plate rotation, such as eastern Papua New Guinea, and to older subduction zones where the kinematics is poorly assessed. Published: 2026-03-08T09:16:43+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.575 (consider)

Authors: Xinxin Wang; Liang Zhao; Marco G. Malusà; Weiqiang Ji; Jie Yuan

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012683

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

Abstract Petrological and seismic constraints suggest differential sampling depth for ultrahigh pressure (UHP) metamorphic rocks exposed in different segments of the fossil subduction zone of the Western Alps. However, the mechanisms for the observed differential exhumation remain to be understood. Here, we account for the continental margin subduction within double subduction systems coupled with three‐dimensional (3‐D) geodynamic models to investigate the potential for this phenomenon. We tested two end‐member scenarios of upper‐plate motion, including Adria counterclockwise rotation and divergent motion away from the trench. Results show that both scenarios can produce a unidirectional increase in the metamorphic peak of exhumed UHP rocks along the orogen strike. Only in the simulations where the counterclockwise rotation of the upper plate acts as the primary driver of exhumation did the resulting characteristics of the deep structure fit with those observed in the Western Alps. They include the presence of an exhumed mantle wedge beneath the southern UHP domes and its absence beneath the northern UHP domes. Our results can be exported to other subduction zones where kinematic constraints suggest a component of upper‐plate rotation, such as eastern Papua New Guinea, and to older subduction zones where the kinematics is poorly assessed.

item.title: The lithospheric carbonatite pathway at Bayan Obo: Evidence from gravity and magnetic inversion and its significance for Nb-REE-Fe mineralization
item.dc:creator: Yongkang Chan
Wenna Zhou
Bohu Xu
Jiaqi Dong
Sihati A
Qingyan Tang
item.description: Clarifying the geometry of deep carbonatite-fluid pathways beneath the supergiant Bayan Obo Nb-REE-Fe deposit, and their coupling with mineralization can provide key constraints for establishing models of ore-forming evolution and for predicting deep mineralization. In this study, satellite gravity data and aeromagnetic data are used to invert the lithospheric-scale density and magnetic susceptibility structures of the Bayan Obo region. The results show that a southward-dipping pathway system rising from the mantle is identified beneath the ore district. The results are in agreement with previously published magnetotelluric inversion profiles. Indication by multiple methods in combination that this system is characterized by the coincidence of high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, and low-resistivity anomalies. Curie depth estimates reveal pronounced Curie surface uplifts in the ore district and its surrounding areas. Together with the high-density, high-magnetic-susceptibility, low-resistivity anomaly belt, these features indicate a persistent deep thermal anomaly. In combination with existing constraints from rock physical property measurements, mineralogical observations, petrological geochemical and geochronological data, this study suggests that Nb-REE-Fe mineralization at Bayan Obo is controlled by a deep carbonatite pathway system. This system was initiated during Mesoproterozoic rifting and was repeatedly reactivated during Mesoproterozoic magmatism (∼1.3 Ga), Early Paleozoic fluid activity (∼0.44 Ga), and Permian granitic magmatism (∼0.27 Ga). This study provides new geophysical evidence for finely characterizing the geometry of deep carbonatite pathways beneath Bayan Obo and their control on multi-stage Nb-REE-Fe mineralization, and offers a reference for deep exploration of similar carbonatite-type Nb-REE-Fe deposits. Published: 2026-03-11T07:45:20+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.575 (consider)

Authors: Yongkang Chan; Wenna Zhou; Bohu Xu; Jiaqi Dong; Sihati A; Qingyan Tang

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration
